meta.definition_of_recurring: A position Paul states in >= 2 distinct articles.
meta.definition_of_emerging: A substantive position stated in exactly one article. 'Single-article' refers to the position, not the article: several come from articles that also feed recurring positions but make a distinct claim. These are curated, not exhaustive.
meta.provenance: Every claim, example and tool verdict is backed by a quote mechanically verified as an exact verbatim substring of out/raw/<source>.txt. Extractions whose quote could not be found in the source were discarded. No position was inferred or generalised beyond what the quoted text states.

positions.claim: AI-detection tools do not work, and institutions that rely on them do more harm than good.

evidence.quote: I wish I had better news, but they don’t work as well as we need them to in order to “trust” them, but it is good to know what is out there.
Sometimes we want a solution so badly that we will collectively see it when it isn't even there.
I would be heartily skeptical of sending my kids there (or any university that uses it or GPTZero or any one of these packages) given the overwhelming evidence that none of these packages work.
AI detection tools (like GPTZero ) don’t work. And if you rely on them exclusively to detect the presence of AI then they can do more harm than good.
it is still difficult to detect if content is AI generated, no matter what law you put in place.
This renders their achievement largely useless, because if the image was created by different software then it generally won’t be flagged.

examples.summary: UCT discontinued its Turnitin AI detection software in disgrace.
examples.quote: The university where I went (UCT) just discontinued using their AI detection software Turnitin in disgrace.

examples.summary: OpenAI's detector has 98.8% success on DALL-E 3 images but struggles with other systems.
examples.quote: It has a 98.8% success rate in identifying images produced by DALL-E 3, though it struggles with detecting media created by other systems.

tools_rated.quote: any university that uses it or GPTZero or any one of these packages) given the overwhelming evidence that none of these packages work.
AI detection tools (like GPTZero ) don’t work. And if you rely on them exclusively to detect the presence of AI then they can do more harm than good.
This is a tool that can identify fake portrait videos. The way it does it is pretty amazing.

positions.note: Stance evolved: an early post relayed GPTZero as a helpful conference recommendation; later posts reject detection tools outright. See tools_rated.GPTZero.stance_changed.

positions.claim: Newsrooms, universities and African institutions should build and own their own models in-house rather than rent someone else's.

evidence.quote: we need an African llm for newsrooms to use internally that is open weights and modified.

The solution I have been touting is simple and I think effective: build an LLM for your campus, tailor it specifically with the books and materials that your students need and allow them to use it as much as they want.
I am busy building these private LLMs for certain organisations and believe universities in Africa need them immediately.
The focus should be on bringing your AI systems in-house and reallocating your saved time (thanks to AI) appropriately.
For African media people the value is going to be in building the models on the continent, keeping them open source and controlling what we feed into them.

I want MediaMap (our app that is mapping the world media’s implementation of AI) to not constantly call the OpenAI API to generate insights and chat responses, because every time it does I have to throw Sam Altman a few coins.

examples.summary: A global Mac mini shortage as people install the OpenClaw AI agent to run AI locally.
examples.quote: Apple announced that there is a global shortage of Mac minis because people are hoovering them up and installing a new AI agent called OpenClaw onto the Mac minis

examples.summary: CITE spent US$10,000 in a year researching and figuring out how to use AI for its newsroom.
examples.quote: He said CITE spent US$10 ,000 this year conducting research and figuring out how to use AI.

tools_rated.quote: By using Ollama you can load a host of LLMs directly onto your computer. This means you can have an AI to use that isn’t connected to the Internet for “sensitive” tasks.
at the moment this model of Llama operates like a broken version of ChatGPT, but you have to start somewhere.

positions.claim: Using AI to cut costs and shed staff is self-defeating; use it to make the work better, with humans kept in the loop.

evidence.quote: some newsrooms are going to take the race to the bottom with the adoption of AI and they are going to suffer for that.
newsrooms, instead of cutting costs, are going to have to use AI, retain their staff and aim to make their content way, way better to be able to charge or attract anyone.
it will help you with your job, but won’t do everything for you.
And the lesson I’ve derived is: use AI for the jobs you can’t easily do, while so many are using AI for tasks that they can do already.
the future is certainly a less glitzy mix of people and emerging tech blended together. So, expect to see drivers in F1 cars maybe forever, but for their jobs to get infinitely easier going forward.
it is easier to have a “big red button” approach and make the whole project AI dependent, but that doesn’t necessarily bring the best results.
a golden lesson for journalists that I try to impress on them is don’t use AI when you can do the job better yourself, especially if it introduces a high risk of creating errors in the process.

examples.summary: The Abu Dhabi autonomous F1 race stopped prematurely, exposing AI's limitations to spectators.
examples.quote: The race ground to a premature stop, with thousands of spectators witnessing the limitations of AI. It was a stark reminder of the formidable challenge of replicating human intuition and reflexes.

examples.summary: Apple AI declared event winners before events happened and outed people who aren't gay.
examples.quote: It is declaring winners of events that haven’t even happened and outing people who aren’t gay.

tools_rated.quote: Straight out of the gate Apple “Intelligence” was all promise and no delivery.
Microsoft’s Copilot is obsessed with giving out links as if it is Google instead of doing what I asked

positions.claim: The AI industry was built on unpaid scraping of other people's work, and creators must be paid for it — collectively, on something like a radio-royalty model.

evidence.quote: The house of AI is built on a foundation of illegal copyright-infringing behaviour which makes the whole situation a Mad Max desert of legal confusion.
by stripping the bylines from their articles and eating them up to create their AI product, OpenAI may have royally exposed themselves to legal tomfoolery that they may not even have been aware of.
The hope is that more outlets come forward and when I saw “more” I mean ALL.
I would say that this is too little, too late and basic lip service and good PR for OpenAI after gobbling up oceans of data to train their model for free.
it was delicious when a company that has built its premium product by stealing the images, audio, text and video created by others then starts to moan about another company stealing from them.
Think of how magazines got obliterated by Google and social media because they settled for scraps when they should have been using lawyers to keep the whole meal. Let’s not see that happen again.
Because realistically these companies that produce content should be able to participate in the upside.

a radio station sends cash to artists every year for being able to play their music on the air and so we need to take that model to AI scraping and force the companies into an agreement so they pay for every scrape, before it’s too late.
OpenAI’s attitude is that of a creative mafia: they will come and ask nicely, maybe even offer you a couple of coins, but if you decline they will simply take what they want

examples.summary: Perplexity caught scraping Forbes' journalism without credit, ignoring the Robots Exclusion Protocol.
examples.quote: Perplexity used Forbes' journalism without credit and ignored the Robots Exclusion Protocol (a standard which sounds cool, but is just a way for websites to tell web crawlers that they can’t be accessed or scanned).

examples.summary: OpenAI deals with media ranging from $5 million for the Financial Times to $250 million for News Corp.
examples.quote: these deals with OpenAI are ranging from estimates of $5 million (for The Financial Times) to $250 million (for News Corp)

tools_rated.quote: A quick test of Suno AI should be all it takes to find them guilty.

positions.claim: Generative AI reproduces a white worldview, and the bias must be filtered at source rather than left for individual users to spot.

evidence.quote: This scandal has proven that black racism in AI is given a shrug and white racism is an international story.
Of course, this is because the scraped web data that AI systems are trained on is incredibly prejudiced.
It proved very difficult to stop Dall-E 3 from always serving up an image of a white person explaining AI to a group of black Ethiopians. Attempt after attempt brought up the same racial bias.
Generative AI is largely serving up a white person’s view of the world.
The scary part for me is it leaves the onus to spot bias on the creator.

And I think that is similarly true for these images, because you can spot bias when it involves your own situation, but the confidence of these models means that someone unwittingly sitting in a different country creating images of places he or she has never been to aren’t going to know the bias or stereotype is even there.

examples.summary: AI image models placed white teachers in authority over students of colour in Ethiopia tests.
examples.quote: It would invariably generate a scenario with a white teacher in authority and the students (often on the floor) would always be people of colour.

examples.summary: ChatGPT-generated bias-illustration image whose faces were 95% white.
examples.quote: The irony is… the faces in the resulting image were 95% white. So, I didn’t use it.

tools_rated.quote: And no version of the image showed a black person in a position of authority explaining to a group of white people. I had to explicitly mention race and explain that I didn’t want white people in the image for them to go away.

It took over a dozen tries to remove African beads from the picture. They would either be on the man’s neck or wrists. Even when ChatGPT said that it had removed the beads they were still in the picture.

positions.claim: African languages are being left behind by the AI ecosystem, and media should actively participate in the language work rather than wait.

evidence.quote: Also, the need for more African languages to be incorporated into the AI ecosystem is crucial.

Language is at the centre of the AI boom and that essentially means English (with a dash of French) are being prioritised. Minority languages, particularly in Africa, are in danger of being left behind.
It is better for the media to be actively involved, generate cash and produce a far better product.
whoever cracks this language issue in Africa will have a very prosperous start-up on their hands

examples.summary: Mallick built a Chichewa ChatGPT whose religious training data biased it toward biblical language.
examples.quote: he used this to train his model and it meant that a bias manifested: the LLM used more “biblical” language. But it also turned the LLM into being more tolerant and philosophical, he says.

positions.claim: Newsrooms should abandon chasing web traffic and stop competing with AI answers for clicks.

evidence.quote: We need to give up casual traffic. People clicking through from LLM questioning is not a sustainable model going forward.
but I don’t think newsrooms should be fighting to appear in LLMs for a few cents.
Accept that traffic to your website is no longer the goal and redesign your work around relationships instead.
Basically, Google is going to saw off its own arm 127 hours style in order to save their business.
the sooner newsrooms abandon simple stories and stop competing with the Google AI overview the better.
this journalism content is no longer your most valuable asset of a newsroom. It is a loss leader.
When I read a press release from The Atlantic it feels like they think ChatGPT is a search engine… it is not. If I’m on ChatGPT I am engaged in a conversation.

examples.summary: Prediction that search-engine web traffic could fall 25% by 2026.
examples.quote: Predictions suggest web traffic from search engines could drop 25% by 2026.

positions.claim: The real AI payoff is in your own data, archives and unglamorous infrastructure — not in generating content.

evidence.quote: The biggest gains from AI doesn’t always come from flashy tools, but from fixing invisible infrastructure. This means clean data, consistent metadata and usable archives.

many independent newsrooms, particularly in countries such as Zimbabwe and Zambia, wrongly think AI is about creating headlines and images, when the bigger opportunity lies in using newsroom data, archives and expertise to build services audiences may actually pay for.
I feel that this types of application is where AI is headed rather than the work of generating listicles that we have become so enthralled by.
I’ve largely been preoccupied with how AI will influence the creation of content, where it feels destructive, but for importing and exporting it looks incredible
if you are a much smaller company then the benefits are going to be exponential. You are suddenly going to be able to process huge amounts of data that could seriously improve your business.
The most exciting part of the AI revolution, for me, is that we will finally have order and insights from all this data we have been collecting.
I have seen a huge gap in using AI technology to document their AI journeys and produce insights based on their data for how they can do better

examples.summary: The newsroom Briefly was nominated for a WAN-IFRA award for the AI assistant Paul helped build.
examples.quote: This included the newsroom Briefly , who were recently nominated for a WAN-IFRA Digital Media Award in the Best AI-driven News Product, Format or Strategy category as a result of the prototype we built together, an AI assistant called EditorialEye.

examples.summary: iHubOnline (Malawi) using AI to make government budget documents accessible.
examples.quote: They are focusing on using AI to interrogate government budget documents (which are hard to understand for most) and making them accessible to all.

tools_rated.quote: it can fully revolutionise your AI work. Airtable can serve as the backbone for your AI projects.
Google’s Journalist Studio and particularly Pinpoint , at least for now, is intent on making your work more efficient.

positions.claim: Vibe-coding and no-code are oversold; the output is janky and you still need to understand the code underneath.

evidence.quote: But coding knowledge is still going to be useful, because it will let you work outside of what these companies want you to do with their tools.
The way for our products and services to stay unique is to embrace the coding.
I am slowly becoming less convinced of the vibe coding hype, because since the acceleration of these platforms to "help" people like me code, my output of apps that actually work has decreased.
attribute 40% to hype, use the tool, but still expect to do at least half the work (while you are being promised a world where you do nothing).
you still need someone who has real knowledge to check the apps before they ship
The coding platforms purport that you can just tell the AI in a paragraph the app you want and it will appear. This is not the case.
The most striking “progression” of vibe-coding is that it now adds dozens of features without you asking and fills graphs with dummy copy.
my fascination with the no-code phenomenon which is naturally enticing but whiffs of snake oil
You give a prompt to fix an error and it regenerates a whole section of code from scratch, fixing the error perhaps, but then deleting a different feature that you were perfectly happy with.

in another year this could be incredible. You can throw up your basic idea with these platforms in a way that is magical, but getting the app functional without diving into the code (they gracefully offer editing as “free”) is impossible
This hybrid option is still the route to go for coding with AI in 2025.

examples.summary: Burning through $20 of Bolt credits in an afternoon as the app filled with errors and crashed.
examples.quote: by working the chatbot hard for an afternoon I fully burned through my $20 of credits allocated to me for the month. The cash ran out just as the app completely filled with errors and crashed

examples.summary: Research showing developers feel faster with AI but are actually slower.
examples.quote: New research shows developers think AI makes them 20% faster but in reality they are 19% slower.

tools_rated.quote: I exported my code into Cursor (which I have raved about continuously in this newsletter). This platform is “AI Code” rather than “no-code” and by comparison feels deeply technical.
My vibe-coding platform of choice is Cursor (using Claude-4.5 Opus).

pinpointing an error is easy, but fixing it economically in a no-code environment is similar to asking ChatGPT to make a very specific change to an image. It’s very tough to get the exact response that you want
vibe coding platforms like Loveable and Replit. Vibe coding (which I adore doing) has created apps that are janky
OpenAI billed me just over $10 to train my mini-model. But when asked to write a newsletter on AI in 2026 it came out a garbled mess.

positions.claim: Trust in images and video is collapsing, and WhatsApp is where that collapse does the most damage.

evidence.quote: Verification is actually going to become more taxing, mainly because of the deluge of AI generated disinformation that is going to be increasingly dumped on us.
The reality of looking at any WhatsApp group and accepting what we see at face value is already over.
if we lose trust in everything we see and are forced to question constantly then how long will we stay engaged?
as soon as this tool hits the market the luxury of believing anything on one of your WhatsApp groups at face value is over. Forever.
They are adding real-time AI image generation to WhatsApp - if you were worried about visual disinformation on the platform before, it is about to go stratospheric.
these integrations mean everything you see on social media is about to be way more dubious than ever before

examples.summary: Katy Perry's AI deepfakes at the Met Gala fooled people including her own mother.
examples.quote: Katy Perry was not at the event despite her likeness being in several photos… she even LIKED the AI generated images on X.

examples.summary: The AI Will Smith eating spaghetti video that seemed absurd only a year earlier.
examples.quote: the AI generated Will Smith where he eats spaghetti like a Men In Black monster made us all scoff in disgust… only a year ago.

tools_rated.quote: when it comes to coding, the new platform absolutely stinks
I tried to make a static podcast cover this week with ChatGPT and though the image I created was interesting, it couldn’t redo it and make a small adjustment to the size of the picture.

positions.claim: Journalists must adopt AI tools and report critically on the AI companies at the same time.

evidence.quote: this dependency could chill the necessary journalism to hold these companies and systems to account.

The analogy I tell people is you can drive a car in your city and still criticise the state of the roads. I guide newsrooms not just to use AI, but also to report on it critically in their reporting work.
it is true that journalists need to cover the horrific developments of AI while actually adopting AI methods and tools to improve their output.

examples.summary: Politico released AI products that were inaccurate and undermined its journalism.
examples.quote: Turns out they have been releasing AI products that don’t work (inaccurate a large amount of the time) and undermine the journalism in the process.

positions.claim: Pro-transparency on regulation, but sceptical it can be enforced — the EU AI Act is a mixed blessing and a poor fit for Africa.

evidence.quote: I’m all for regulation and transparency around the material these models use, but I’m not convinced with how we are going to enforce the rules once they are in place.
Africa needs to regulate away the harm, but make sure that they harness the benefits so their economies don’t get left behind.
I’m not for these types of products, but it feels like it is wide open to interpretation.

Those under scrutiny as "unacceptable" are AI systems used for social scoring, real-time facial recognition in public and manipulative systems that prey on the vulnerable. If your model distorts behaviour and causes harm, it’s banned. Fair enough.
I am for this in theory, we have already seen rampant bias and racism in areas like recruitment, but arguably the red tape could stop plenty of innovations even happening.

I’ve said before that the AI Act is grossly pro government: go back to that face recognition rule, it stops security companies from developing systems but allows the police to create their own. If you move this to an African context, where services are increasingly privatised, it’s ludicrous.
The big LLMs will need to disclose their training data, comply with copyright law, and publish how their models work. This is great news.
the Commission is considering giving a one-year “grace period” for high-risk or generative AI systems already on the market. One year in AI is like ten or a hundred regular years.

Delaying the AI Act may not necessarily fix its problems. The regulation’s architecture is already showing strain: the original sector-based risk logic collapsed in the face of general-purpose models like ChatGPT

examples.summary: Projected AI economic benefit for four African nations by 2030.
examples.quote: Nigeria, Ghana, Kenya, and South Africa alone could see up to $136 billion injected into their economies by 2030 thanks to AI.

examples.summary: Jim Acosta interviewed an AI avatar of a Parkland shooting victim.
examples.quote: Joaquin Oliver, who died at age 17 in the Parkland school shooting in 2018 was interviewed by Acosta on Tuesday with the "magic" of AI and the endorsement of the kid/now adult’s father, Manuel.

positions.claim: News-gathering, idea generation and the writing itself should stay human.

evidence.quote: Though, personally, I believe this stuff has a long way to go in order to replace anyone.
My pitch is that both of these should be left uncontaminated by AI.
while 90% of newsrooms are using AI for idea generation, leave that up to your humans.
then it is worth the effort for writers to write and to give them the chance of spark and creativity and something new.
I wanted to work through my own thoughts on the topic by writing them down. I am not sure that will happen to the world if we all stop doing this.

The pleasure and the craft of producing a product without AI has already been lost once you have even used it a little and the joy, at least for me, when using these tools is to see what can be produced with the least amount of effort.

what is going to hold back huge amounts of content becoming generative isn’t just how the audience reacts, but because people LIKE creating. And people (including me) will probably keep writing or editing to their financial detriment long past the point where they have to.

examples.summary: OpenAI's $10 fine-tune of Paul's newsletter model produced garbled output.
examples.quote: OpenAI billed me just over $10 to train my mini-model. But when asked to write a newsletter on AI in 2026 it came out a garbled mess.

examples.summary: Paul's script that generates a ten-minute Johannesburg news podcast with three synthetic voices.
examples.quote: I managed to get a working script together that would spit out a complete ten minute podcast episode recounting the daily news from Johannesburg, in a discussion format between three trained imaginary voices.

tools_rated.quote: NotebookLM is free and so pushes back against that problem, however, the more existential question is are we comfortable with these chatty AI hosts created by NotebookLM giving us information?

positions.claim: The membership / engagement / 'financial sustainability' orthodoxy is a dead end for journalism.

evidence.quote: Content is no longer scarce and so people aren’t going to pay for it. Good impactful content is rare but people won’t pay for it because they can just consume something else that is free.

It is not sensical for us to talk about financial sustainability as being a measure of success for media when a willingness for people to pay for something has never been a measure of the quality of a piece of content
Media organisations have become largely brainwashed by large donors and impact investors to prioritise “membership models” and “audience engagement” as the road to revenue.
though there are success stories of membership models making money, by design it can only work for the top few. The rest are left berating themselves and agonising over their newsletter open rates.

If you are in a death spiral of only caring about engagement, let me suggest you move into a different industry. You do not own YouTube. If you think a successful media entity is about “engagement on the platform” then I feel maybe you have wandered into the wrong room.
It isn’t just the business model of journalism that is broken, it is the business model of being a journalist that has always been precarious.

examples.summary: The Daily Sun's print circulation collapsed over ten years.
examples.quote: The tabloid township paper, the Daily Sun, has dropped from 283,216 to 11,889 in circulation in the last ten years (thanks to The Outlier for the figures).

positions.note: Only one verbatim supporting example was found in the corpus for this position; it is drawn from a different article than the position quotes.

positions.claim: Discount the hype heavily: don't chase each new model, and distrust optimistic reports written by companies profiting from AI.

evidence.quote: 2024 is truly going to be the year when we see the hype of AI settle and start to understand whose work is really in danger.
Innovation is not just doing the new shiny thing automatically.

GPT-5 is coming ... but there is a good argument for why you shouldn’t care. We are caught in a AI hype cycle of the major players endlessly releasing new models that will keep promising to improve your life.
attribute 40% to hype, use the tool, but still expect to do at least half the work (while you are being promised a world where you do nothing).
Expect more of this type of biased pandering from the people profiting from this tech in the months to come.

examples.summary: Research showing developers feel faster with AI but are actually slower.
examples.quote: New research shows developers think AI makes them 20% faster but in reality they are 19% slower.

examples.summary: ChatGPT price rise caused 25-30% of users to cancel, illustrating startup fragility.
examples.quote: When ChatGPT raised prices? 25-30% of users cancelled immediately. What happens when API prices triple. Most of these startups die overnight.

positions.claim: Be honest with audiences about where AI was used, and every organisation needs an ethical AI policy — sooner is better.

evidence.quote: We should be asking if the AI used is ethical (or at least more ethical than others).
we need to acknowledge where AI is involved in the reporting process and be honest with audiences about what that means.
the ethical guardrails around AI needs to be “sold” to newsrooms as a necessity, really as the only way they are going to stand above every YouTuber and TikToker in the land.
It feels like every organisation needs to go through this process and great results can come for those who do it sooner rather than later.

examples.summary: A Nairobi editor admitted they couldn't disclose AI use for fear audiences would think them lazy.
examples.quote: one editor revealed that they were in a bind. There was no way they could disclose that they were using AI to their audience, because they were convinced that their audience would consider them too “lazy” if they discovered AI was being used.

positions.claim: 'Open source' AI mostly isn't: Llama is open-weight, and Meta releases it to lock developers in.

evidence.quote: After China dropped the DeepSeek bomb last week, Open Source AI has edged closer to being the norm.
it still seems like their strategy is to give away Llama in order to stay relevant and encourage developers to work within the Meta ecosystem.
Let’s be clear: at this point Meta knows it doesn’t have to do this and they want us to be grateful.

Zuck insists on calling Llama 3 “open source” because it is a term people associate with ye olde Internet of good, free and fair. However, it technically isn’t open source. It is “open weight” which is fundamentally different and not as rosy.

examples.summary: Meta AI succeeded at only one of four coding tasks while ChatGPT nailed all four.
examples.quote: ChatGPT nailed all four tasks and Meta AI only succeeded at finding the bug.

examples.summary: Meta AI refused to create an image because the prompt mentioned it was for an article.
examples.quote: Meta AI refused to create an image for the prompt “create an image for the article ‘With AI will Facebook finally improve the world?’” After testing a bunch of variations of the prompt I discovered that it wasn’t “AI” or “Facebook” that it was balking at, but that I was producing an image for an “article”

tools_rated.quote: when it comes to coding, the new platform absolutely stinks
at the moment this model of Llama operates like a broken version of ChatGPT, but you have to start somewhere.
By using Ollama you can load a host of LLMs directly onto your computer. This means you can have an AI to use that isn’t connected to the Internet for “sensitive” tasks.

emerging_positions.claim: When a state deploys a harmful AI system, the AI does the political work of distancing the decision from the people who took it.

evidence.quote: It is a story about a state knowingly deploying a system that would load the cost of public healthcare onto people who could not pay, while preserving deniability about who made that call.
The AI is doing the political work of distancing the decision from the people who took it.
the next time a government tells you that AI will help it deliver social benefits more fairly, ask whose definition of fair the system was tuned to. Ask what data it was trained on, and when.

examples.summary: Kenya's SHA AI model systematically overcharged the poorest Kenyans and undercharged the wealthiest for health insurance.
examples.quote: They found that the model systematically overcharged the poorest Kenyans for health insurance and undercharged the wealthiest.

examples.summary: IDinsight consultants knew the SHA system was flawed and suggested a complaints system, launching it anyway.
examples.quote: The consultants knew the system was flawed and could not be made reliably accurate, especially for poor people. As a fix they suggested putting a complaints system in place and launching it anyway.

emerging_positions.claim: AI defamation goes unpunished because the cases die for lack of funding while platforms quietly patch the error.

evidence.quote: And without big cash flush individuals who can challenge this stuff it will keep happening without consequences.
Even clear-cut instances of AI defamation can die for want of funding, with platforms quietly fixing errors rather than facing real accountability.
BNN is the clearest example yet of a publisher (not an AI company) being sued for using AI to produce defamatory content

examples.summary: ChatGPT falsely named whistleblower Brian Hood as guilty in a bribery scandal.
examples.quote: Unfortunately ChatGPT (in the early days of the app in March 2023) falsely named him as a guilty party in the scandal, claiming he had served prison time for bribery.

examples.summary: The Dave Fanning case: AI attached his photo to a story about an unnamed broadcaster he had no link to.
examples.quote: the AI filled the gap by attaching a photo of a “prominent Irish broadcaster” (which turned out to be Dave Fanning, the well-known DJ who helped kickstart U2). He had absolutely nothing to do with the case.

emerging_positions.claim: AI's environmental damage will only be curbed by what it costs businesses, not by ethics.

evidence.quote: the real pressure, that might save the environment, is going to be the cost to businesses.
This thrifty nature, at least, could slightly curb the environmental chaos that’s coming.
not every use case needs the model to be working at the highest level, so being able to moderate this will reduce the footprint to a degree.

examples.summary: ChatGPT is estimated to use 500ml of fresh water per 5-50 prompts.
examples.quote: it is estimated that ChatGPT is thirsty for 500ml of fresh water to generate between 5 and 50 prompts or questions.

examples.summary: Training GPT-3 used 700,000 litres of clean freshwater.
examples.quote: training GPT-3 in Microsoft’s state-of-the-art U.S. data centres used 700,000 litres of clean freshwater

emerging_positions.claim: The real leverage lies with the journalists doing the work, and they will not accept being reduced to data collectors for AI agents.

evidence.quote: The profile of what a journalist will be in the future is something I worry about, because I’m not sure a person who is willing to put in the WORK and RISK to get truly interesting stories exists if they can’t actually write or create them.

The tech media development community, which I am now firmly a part of, has not fully realised that the leverage is with the people doing the actual journalism and those journalists haven’t realised that they have the leverage either.
Put it this way, if I was them and the industry changed in this way, I would do something else. Something less risky to my physical safety and more financially viable.

emerging_positions.note: No verbatim supporting example for this position was found in the corpus; the claim rests on the evidence quotes alone.

emerging_positions.claim: Media literacy failed because it carried a smug, elite political agenda that audiences sensed and rejected.

evidence.quote: And people immediately saw the smug, elite attitude behind that. You can be Media Literate and "informed on the issues" and still have voted for Brexit
You can't have a political agenda of what you want a person to believe if you are sincerely trying to teach them to be critical on their own terms.

emerging_positions.note: No verbatim supporting example for this position was found in the corpus; the claim rests on the evidence quotes alone.

emerging_positions.claim: Disney suing Midjourney is not a crusade for creators but a powerful IP owner attacking an underdog; big IP owners should monetise their IP for derivative works instead.

evidence.quote: Having a merciless IP factory suing a relative tech underdog (Midjounrey is not at the scale of OpenAI) is not a crusade for The Creators.
surely they would want to monetise the IP so the public could still create “derivative works” from their content, but they see the upside.

emerging_positions.note: No verbatim supporting example for this position was found in the corpus; the claim rests on the evidence quotes alone.

emerging_positions.claim: By funding public deepfake literacy, OpenAI and Microsoft shift the problem onto users instead of fixing it themselves.

evidence.quote: when their platform is helping to generate the fake content, then it feels like they are saying that it is our problem to sift through this mess, rather than theirs.

examples.summary: AI deepfake images of Rihanna at the Met Gala went viral though she had flu and didn't attend.
examples.quote: images of her got millions of views as she smiled into camera while wearing a dress covered in birds and flowers. Turns out, she had flu and didn’t attend.

emerging_positions.claim: Selling or handing over your voice cheaply means giving up a major part of your IP and your vocal privacy.

evidence.quote: I have been worrying about voice cloning for a year now. And nothing that has happened in the past 12 months has dampened those fears.
what has concerned me more is how readily we are willing to give over our voice samples to paid services like ElevenLabs (me included) to clone our own voices to make our lives more convenient.
If you wilfully document and sell your voice for scraps then surely you are giving up a major part of your IP?

examples.summary: Amazon sued for cloning actors' voices to finish the Road House remake.
examples.quote: Amazon is being sued for cloning the voices of actors during the strike last year. They allegedly did it to get the production of the new Road House remake wrapped.

examples.summary: Steven Bartlett cloning his own voice to release AI podcasts.
examples.quote: Steven “King of Slop” Bartlett who is cloning his own voice to release AI podcasts of him blabbing on about CEOs.

emerging_positions.claim: Journalism content is now a loss leader: newsrooms should make slow news faster and build paid utilities alongside the content.

evidence.quote: AI offers opportunities for newsrooms to create utilities (separate from their content) that their audience will be more willing to pay for.
instead of making more fast news, I would attempt to make slow news faster.
I am encouraging newsrooms globally to build audience clones with AI, even if just inside ChatGPT.

examples.summary: A Zimbabwean agriculture newsroom building an AI to substitute for absent government extension officers.
examples.quote: this newsroom is going to build an AI system, based on their data, that can be a substitute for these officers when they are not available.

emerging_positions.claim: Hallucinated links and sources are the biggest shortcoming of using LLMs for research, and nobody was held accountable when it reached national policy.

evidence.quote: This proclivity to hallucinate links and sources is the most glaring shortcoming of using LLMs for research.
the fact that Minister Malatsi still has a job after this weekend is dumbfounding.
they want their staff to sweat and care for the articles they produce and AI isn’t taking that away from them, but it is giving them an opportunity to expose their apathy.

examples.summary: South Africa's Draft National AI Policy was AI-generated and full of fabricated references.
examples.quote: South Africa’s Draft National AI Policy was generated (at least partly) with AI and as a result is full of fabricated references ( News24 found at least six).

emerging_positions.claim: Only high-impact, donor-funded journalism is worth producing; broadcasters and donors should stop paying for fluff that machines can now make endlessly.

evidence.quote: the future of all journalism that has deep impact is donor funded. And that is all journalists should be producing. All the rest is noise.
No one needs the BBC to write movie reviews.
All other content should be ceased and donors should put pressure on those they are giving money to to stop with the fluff. We have machines that can create fluff all day now.

emerging_positions.note: No verbatim supporting example for this position was found in the corpus; the claim rests on the evidence quotes alone.

emerging_positions.claim: Automating journalists' work requires a guarantee that nobody is fired for revealing what can be automated, plus internal metrics proving AI actually improved the product.

evidence.quote: there needs to be a trust between the journalists and the bosses to say that no one is going to be retrenched once the journalist reveals their tasks and brainstorms how to automate them.
you want a company’s internal metrics to judge how the product is getting better through the AI adoption process.

emerging_positions.note: No verbatim supporting example for this position was found in the corpus; the claim rests on the evidence quotes alone.

ratings.quote: I slagged off Adobe’s Enhance Speech tool a few weeks ago (it is meant to turn dodgy voice over recordings into studio quality) and I have since discovered that it does work, but only on the paid tier.
It does not work on any level. Stripping out breaths where you need them and making your voice overly robotic. We are still a way off.
it can fully revolutionise your AI work. Airtable can serve as the backbone for your AI projects.
Straight out of the gate Apple “Intelligence” was all promise and no delivery.
Their new AI move ReALM may actually reverse Siri’s lobotomy and make it useful.

I have discovered a site called Better Images of AI (thanks to Peter Deselaers) which is on a mission to depict AI in the media (and in newsletters like this one) with fewer “unhelpful” stereotypes. It’s worth checking out.

pinpointing an error is easy, but fixing it economically in a no-code environment is similar to asking ChatGPT to make a very specific change to an image. It’s very tough to get the exact response that you want
I tried putting all my conference notes into ChatGPT to write the main section above and the TONE was incredibly grating. I didn’t use anything that it gave me.
ChatGPT will take your to-do-list and build a comprehensive plan, particularly if you tell it your working hours and deadlines.

It was only after I told ChatGPT that it had been a disaster did it pick out those negative facts and include them in the story. As always, I had to rewrite the article to remove the generic “ChatGPT voice” that has emerged in the last year.
ChatGPT is brilliant at summarising huge amounts of text, but it has a distinct voice and a proclivity for cliches and repeating certain phrases. It did not sound like me at all.
I tried to make a static podcast cover this week with ChatGPT and though the image I created was interesting, it couldn’t redo it and make a small adjustment to the size of the picture.

It took over a dozen tries to remove African beads from the picture. They would either be on the man’s neck or wrists. Even when ChatGPT said that it had removed the beads they were still in the picture.
the GPTs inside ChatGPT that claim they can transcribe are mostly just chatbots that advise you on which services you could use if you paid money
Though ChatGPT-4o is mind blowing for code, its voice is becoming more distinct when asking it to write and this is making it less useful for creating written content.
I’ve been trying alternatives to ChatGPT. Anthropic's chatbot Claude 2 is free and friendly.
However, Cursor is changing my coding life. It takes over Visual Studio Code (where I hammer away coding Python) and adds an AI helper right into your workflow.
I exported my code into Cursor (which I have raved about continuously in this newsletter). This platform is “AI Code” rather than “no-code” and by comparison feels deeply technical.
My vibe-coding platform of choice is Cursor (using Claude-4.5 Opus).
DALL·E (embedded into ChatGPT) is gradually improving (and was used to produce the images in this article).

And no version of the image showed a black person in a position of authority explaining to a group of white people. I had to explicitly mention race and explain that I didn’t want white people in the image for them to go away.

I transcribed the video of the Columbia panel using Descript because the GPTs inside ChatGPT that claim they can transcribe are mostly just chatbots that advise you on which services you could use if you paid money.
The truth is I upgraded my plan to access the AI features and my voice clone sounded robotic and nothing like me. It is a waste of time.
this is similar to Descript for audio editing: useful to startt an edit but the full control you need to do a final polish is largely absent from AI tools
Eleven Labs is by far the best AI text to voice service on the market (I used it in my podcast AI tool described above).
The futile silliness of this being useful when you consider the rapid disinformation cesspool of platforms like WhatsApp is laughable.
My verification failed so many times that I had to contact them directly and ask them to give me more attempts.
though it was thin on citations (and I fed it a topic about my favourite movie) I was shocked with how it took just a title and tied an argument together.

Fireflies.ai doesn’t just transcribe your Zoom conversations, but compiles pretty competent summaries and sends them out to everyone who was on the call. It really works, but the paid version needs to be cheaper.
One recommendation from Meyer I found very helpful was GPTZero . It’s an AI-detection tool. It is trained to detect ChatGPT, GPT4, Bard, LLaMa, and other AI models.
any university that uses it or GPTZero or any one of these packages) given the overwhelming evidence that none of these packages work.
AI detection tools (like GPTZero ) don’t work. And if you rely on them exclusively to detect the presence of AI then they can do more harm than good.
This is a little route one, but Google’s Colab is the place to start when coding.
The truth is generating full length video with Veo 3 is too expensive for a hobbyist and not professional enough for real production.
Google’s Gemini is still hobbled by not being allowed to create pictures of people

Google Looker Studio (previously known as Google Data Studio) is the Google Docs of data visualisation. It integrates with all your other Google tools and lets you quickly turn your data into a cool looking graph or dashboard.
Google’s Journalist Studio and particularly Pinpoint , at least for now, is intent on making your work more efficient.
This is a tool that can identify fake portrait videos. The way it does it is pretty amazing.
at the moment this model of Llama operates like a broken version of ChatGPT, but you have to start somewhere.
vibe coding platforms like Loveable and Replit. Vibe coding (which I adore doing) has created apps that are janky
Luma Labs have beat OpenAI to it with Dream Machine . It is astounding.
Meedan’s Check is a fantastic service where you can build a tip line for your community and analyse the results.
when it comes to coding, the new platform absolutely stinks
Microsoft’s Copilot is obsessed with giving out links as if it is Google instead of doing what I asked
Midjourney is still the image generating product to beat, particularly as Google’s Gemini is still hobbled by not being allowed to create pictures of people
I have finally migrated to Midjourney. I used it to created the picture above and though it is fiddly to run through Discord (and there is no free tier) I am never going back.
The quality of a Midjourney pic is getting better, but the “intelligence” of the model seems to be waning, in terms of responding accurately to my prompts.
For the picture this week I used Midjourney and avoided any mention of AI. But I did use phrases like “hyper realistic photo”, “award-winning photography”
NotebookLM is free and so pushes back against that problem, however, the more existential question is are we comfortable with these chatty AI hosts created by NotebookLM giving us information?
NotebookLM is already an app I use to absorb a bunch of complicated documents in a short space of time (via 20 minutes of audio rather than read 100s of pages)
If you are bored of the arbitrarily flat tone of ChatGPT then try NovelAI for story inspiration. Though, personally, I believe this stuff has a long way to go in order to replace anyone.
By using Ollama you can load a host of LLMs directly onto your computer. This means you can have an AI to use that isn’t connected to the Internet for “sensitive” tasks.
The OpenAI Cookbook is an incredible resource of papers, videos and guides to help you write killer prompts.
OpenAI billed me just over $10 to train my mini-model. But when asked to write a newsletter on AI in 2026 it came out a garbled mess.

The first few are certainly impressive, particularly with how it has identified the key points from an hour of video and added subtitles automatically, but it struggles with trying to translate Arabic and the last couple are nonsensical.
While apps like Otter.ai and Perplexity are being exposed as either immoral data hoards or copyright pirates
Perplexity is still great, being essentially a chatty search engine.
But the new Rode PodMic USB is dynamite.
Runway are an AI company focused on art and entertainment based in New York. They are an inspiration.
Shodan feels futuristic and frightening as it allows you to search for anything connected to the Internet from power plants to refrigerators to open servers
The other obvious addition to Spotify is the AI DJ that has a parody Grand Theft Auto voice and does little but explain why it is playing the next set of songs
Despite me trashing Spotify’s DJ, I am busy migrating a few pods over to Spotify For Podcasters . It is a great hosting service and, of course, free.
I’m a huge fan of The Ringer Podcast Network and Bill Simmons (who sold to Spotify in 2020 and consulted on this process). Here are the examples done so far. It is absolutely bananas.

now I am using a free program called Visual Studio Code , because it is a tad more stable and it is easier to shift your app from your machine to the cloud
